steps.title: Gastroenterology and food allergy
steps.text: Multiple Choice for Secondary Care

steps.title: Chronic Abdominal Pain
steps.text: What is the appropriate next step for managing a child with chronic abdominal pain at the secondary level?
steps.rationale: At the secondary level, the focus is on performing appropriate investigations (labs, imaging, stool tests, etc.) to find the underlying cause before escalating care.

textChoices.text: Conduct relevant investigations to identify an underlying cause
Reassure the family and schedule a yearly follow-up

steps.title: Chronic Abdominal Pain
steps.text: When should a child with chronic abdominal pain be referred from secondary to tertiary care?
steps.rationale: If no clear diagnosis is reached after appropriate workup at secondary level, referral to tertiary care is warranted for specialist input and advanced diagnostics.

textChoices.text: If symptoms are controlled with diet changes
If stool test shows pinworm infestation
If no underlying cause is found despite appropriate investigations

steps.title: Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease
steps.text: Which is the first-line drug for treating GERD in children at the secondary level?
steps.rationale: PPIs like omeprazole are the recommended first-line pharmacologic option.

steps.title: Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease
steps.text: Which drug class should not be used routinely for pediatric GERD?
steps.rationale: Prokinetics (e.g. domperidone, erythromycin) are not routinely recommended due to limited evidence and potential side effects.

steps.title: Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease
steps.text: What is the goal when prescribing GERD medication?
steps.rationale: The aim is to use minimal effective dosing for the shortest time to manage symptoms.

textChoices.text: Use the lowest effective dose briefly

steps.title: Peptic Ulcer Disease (PUD)
steps.text: Which of the following is a red flag symptom that warrants referral for possible PUD?
steps.rationale: Hematemesis and involuntary weight loss are clear red flags suggesting gastritis or PUD, requiring urgent referral.

steps.title: Peptic Ulcer Disease (PUD)
steps.text: What is the first-line pharmacologic treatment for confirmed PUD in older children at the secondary level?
steps.rationale: PPIs (omeprazole, esomeprazole) are the first-line treatment in older children, given at 1–2 mg/kg/day.

steps.title: Peptic Ulcer Disease (PUD)
steps.text: When should H. pylori eradication therapy be initiated in a child with PUD?
steps.rationale: H. pylori treatment should be started only when infection is confirmed, not empirically.

textChoices.text: Only after confirmation of H. pylori infection
At the first suspicion of abdominal pain
Before any endoscopic or lab confirmation
In all children with nausea and bloating

steps.title: Peptic Ulcer Disease (PUD)
steps.text: Which PUD presentation at the secondary level requires urgent referral?
steps.rationale: All bleeding patients should be referred to tertiary care for urgent evaluation and endoscopic management.

steps.title: Chronic Diarrhea
steps.text: A 2-year-old child presents with chronic diarrhea and signs of wasting. What is an appropriate next step at the secondary care level?
steps.rationale: At the secondary level, it is essential to stabilize the patient, correct dehydration, and begin evaluation for infectious and nutritional causes.

textChoices.text: Correct dehydration and evaluate for malnutrition and infection

steps.title: Chronic Diarrhea
steps.text: When should a child with chronic diarrhea be referred from secondary to tertiary care?
steps.rationale: Persistent symptoms or suspicion of non-infectious or complex causes (e.g. IBD, pancreatic insufficiency, congenital disorders) warrant referral to tertiary care for further investigation.

textChoices.text: If the stool pH is above 7
If stool frequency reduces with fluids
If there is mild weight loss
If diarrhea persists or a non-infectious cause is suspected

steps.title: Chronic Diarrhea
steps.text: Which of the following stool investigations helps differentiate between osmotic and secretory diarrhea?
steps.rationale: The stool osmotic gap helps distinguish osmotic diarrhea (high gap) from secretory diarrhea (gap <50). This guides diagnosis and treatment in persistent or unclear cases.

steps.title: Food allergy
steps.text: A child presents with sudden wheezing, rash, and hypotension after eating peanuts. What is your first priority?
steps.rationale: A child with signs of anaphylaxis requires immediate stabilization using the ABCDE approach and emergency management protocols.

textChoices.text: Initiate ABCDE and manage as anaphylaxis
Give antihistamines and monitor at home
Ask the family to remove peanuts from diet and return if worse

steps.title: Food allergy
steps.text: What is the next best step in a child with recurrent hives after certain meals, but currently stable?
steps.rationale: A thorough history is essential, but all suspected food allergy cases should be referred to higher-level care for further evaluation and testing.

textChoices.text: Take detailed dietary history and refer
Perform skin prick test in primary care
Start an elimination diet without documentation

steps.title: Inflammatory Bowel Disease
steps.text: Which of the following symptom combinations should raise suspicion for IBD in a child?
steps.rationale: Chronic diarrhea, rectal bleeding, and growth retardation are classic features of inflammatory bowel disease, especially Crohn’s disease.

textChoices.text: Fever and isolated upper abdominal pain after meals
Chronic diarrhea with rectal bleeding and growth retardation

steps.title: Inflammatory Bowel Disease
steps.text: Which of the following symptoms is more commonly associated with Crohn’s disease than ulcerative colitis in children?
steps.rationale: Crohn’s disease is more often associated with aphthous ulcers and growth retardation, which may precede GI symptoms and help differentiate it from ulcerative colitis.

textChoices.text: Aphthous oral ulcers and growth failure

steps.title: Coeliac disease
steps.text: A child with undiagnosed coeliac disease presents with severe diarrhea, hypotension, and signs of shock. What is the immediate priority?
steps.rationale: In cases of coeliac crisis, immediate treatment of life-threatening dehydration or anemia is essential before initiating other interventions.

textChoices.text: Stabilize with fluids and treat life-threatening dehydration
Prescribe gluten-free diet and reassess next day
Order HLA typing before any treatment

steps.title: Coeliac disease
steps.text: Which of the following grains is safe for children with coeliac disease to consume?
steps.rationale: Rice is gluten-free and safe. Others like wheat, rye, barley, and spelt contain gluten and must be avoided permanently.

steps.title: Coeliac disease
steps.text: What is an essential component of long-term management for a child with coeliac disease?
steps.rationale: Children with coeliac disease require a lifelong gluten-free diet, even if symptoms resolve, to prevent complications and support growth.

textChoices.text: Gradual reintroduction of gluten after 6 months

steps.title: Acute Liver Failure
steps.text: In a child with acute liver failure and signs of shock, what is the recommended initial fluid resuscitation strategy?
steps.rationale: Volume repletion with normal saline (20 mL/kg bolus, repeat if necessary) is the first-line fluid in hemodynamic compromise due to liver failure.

steps.title: Acute Liver Failure
steps.text: What is the goal when using lactulose to control hyperammonemia in children with acute liver failure?
steps.rationale: Lactulose helps lower ammonia levels by targeting the gut; the goal is 2–4 loose stools/day to ensure effective clearance.

steps.title: Acute Liver Failure
steps.text: What should be done after stabilizing a child with acute liver failure at a secondary facility?
steps.rationale: All children with acute liver failure must be referred to tertiary care after stabilization, as definitive care requires specialist input.

textChoices.text: Refer to a tertiary facility for further management

steps.title: Acute Liver Failure
steps.text: Which of the following supports neuroprotection in a child with hepatic encephalopathy?
steps.rationale: Head elevation helps reduce the risk of increased intracranial pressure, a serious complication of hepatic encephalopathy.

steps.title: Acute Liver Failure
steps.text: Which of the following is a clear indication for early intubation in a child with acute liver failure?
steps.rationale: Rapidly worsening encephalopathy requires early intubation to protect the airway and prevent further neurological compromise.

textChoices.text: Normal ammonia level with mild confusion

steps.title: Acute Liver Failure
steps.text: A child with ALF is hypotensive and poorly responsive to two normal saline boluses. What is the next step?
steps.rationale: If a child with ALF remains hypotensive despite fluid resuscitation, vasopressors should be initiated to support perfusion.

textChoices.text: Delay treatment until renal function tests return

steps.title: Acute Liver Failure
steps.text: Which of the following can be used to treat raised ICP in acute liver failure?
steps.rationale: Mannitol (0.5–1 g/kg) and 3% hypertonic saline are both effective options for reducing intracranial pressure in ALF.

steps.title: Acute Liver Failure
steps.text: What is the recommended treatment for active bleeding due to coagulopathy in acute liver failure?
steps.rationale: Management includes IV vitamin K and FFP (15 mL/kg) for patients with active bleeding.

textChoices.text: IV Vitamin K and FFP transfusion

steps.title: Acute Liver Failure
steps.text: Which medication is indicated in a child with acute liver failure due to acetaminophen overdose?
steps.rationale: N-acetylcysteine is the antidote of choice for paracetamol (acetaminophen) toxicity, and is a cornerstone in ALF related to overdose.

steps.title: Acute Liver Failure
steps.text: Which antibiotic is recommended for prophylaxis against infection in a child with acute liver failure?
steps.rationale: Ceftriaxone (50 mg/kg/day) is the recommended prophylactic antibiotic in acute liver failure due to the high risk of infection and sepsis in these patients.

steps.title: Liver Cirrhosis
steps.text: A 10-year-old with suspected liver cirrhosis presents with confusion and vomiting. What is the most appropriate initial step at a primary or secondary care facility?
steps.rationale: Stabilization and checking for hypoglycemia are key priorities in any child presenting with signs of hepatic decompensation.

textChoices.text: Stabilize the patient and check blood sugar

steps.title: Liver Cirrhosis
steps.text: What is the recommended first-line treatment for coagulopathy-related bleeding in a child with liver cirrhosis?
steps.rationale: Vitamin K (2.5–10 mg/day) is used to manage bleeding due to impaired synthesis of clotting factors in chronic liver disease.

steps.title: Liver Cirrhosis
steps.text: Which of the following actions is appropriate in the care of a child with chronic liver disease at the primary or secondary level?
steps.rationale: Mid-upper arm circumference is useful for nutritional assessment, and palliative care involvement is crucial for chronic or end-stage cases.

textChoices.text: Perform nutritional assessment and refer to palliative care if needed
Delay referral until liver enzymes normalize

steps.title: Portal hypertension
steps.text: A 6-year-old presents with splenomegaly, abdominal distension, and hematemesis. You suspect portal hypertension. What is the most appropriate action at a primary or secondary care facility?
steps.rationale: At the primary or secondary level, the priority is stabilization (manage airway, breathing, circulation) and urgent referral to tertiary care for further diagnostic workup and definitive management of portal hypertension.

textChoices.text: Stabilize the patient and refer to tertiary care
Order a liver biopsy before referring

steps.title: Ascites
steps.text: A 6-year-old presents with moderate ascites. Which of the following is part of the standard initial workup at the secondary care level?
steps.rationale: At the secondary level, key investigations for ascites include abdominal ultrasound and diagnostic paracentesis to evaluate for infection, malignancy, or other causes.

steps.title: Ascites
steps.text: Which of the following investigations is most useful to screen for infectious causes of ascites at the secondary care level?
steps.rationale: To evaluate infectious causes of ascites, tests like blood and urine cultures, and screening for tuberculosis or schistosomiasis, are appropriate and routinely performed at the secondary level.

textChoices.text: Blood and urine cultures, and TB screening
